title: Commemorating the 80th anniversary of the liberation of the Auschwitz extermination camp in Nazi-occupied Poland and International Holocaust Remembrance Day.
plain_summary: This resolution honors the 80th anniversary of when Allied forces freed prisoners from the Auschwitz death camp in Poland during World War II. It also recognizes International Holocaust Remembrance Day, which remembers the millions of Jewish people and others who were killed by the Nazis. The resolution asks all Americans to remember these victims and to keep fighting against hatred toward Jewish people. It also calls on people to work toward ending all forms of violence against groups of people and to promote peace and understanding.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution commemorates the eightieth anniversary of when Allied Forces freed the people imprisoned at the Auschwitz extermination camp in Nazi-occupied Poland. It also recognizes International Holocaust Remembrance Day.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: The United Nations chose January twenty-seventh of each year as International Holocaust Remembrance Day to remember victims of the Holocaust. During World War Two, the Nazi regime and its helpers systematically murdered six million Jews and millions of other people. The Auschwitz extermination camp complex in Nazi-occupied Poland was the largest death camp the Nazi regime built. It included a killing center at Birkenau. On January twenty-seventh, nineteen forty-five, Allied Forces liberated Auschwitz after almost five years of murder, rape, and torture at the camp. Nearly one million three hundred thousand innocent civilians were taken from their homes across Eastern and Western Europe to Auschwitz, especially from Hungary, Poland, and France. Nearly one million one hundred thousand innocent civilians were murdered at Auschwitz between nineteen forty and nineteen forty-five. At least nine hundred sixty thousand of the nearly one million one hundred thousand murdered people were Jewish. The more than one hundred thousand other victims who died at Auschwitz included non-Jewish Poles, Romani people, Soviet civilians and prisoners of war, Afro-Germans, Jehovah's Witnesses, people with disabilities, gay men and women, and other ethnic minorities. These innocent civilians were subjected to torture, forced labor, starvation, rape, medical experiments, and separation from loved ones. The names of many of these innocent individuals who died have been lost forever. The Auschwitz extermination camp represents the extraordinary brutality of the Holocaust. The United States Holocaust Memorial Museum serves as our nation's memorial to Holocaust victims and teaches the history and lessons of the Holocaust to people from all walks of life in the United States. The people of the United States must never forget the genocide and terrible crimes against humanity committed at Auschwitz. The people of the United States must educate future generations to promote understanding of the dangers of intolerance to prevent similar injustices, including acts of violent anti-Semitism, from happening again. There are only approximately two hundred twenty thousand Holocaust survivors who are still living, and it is more important now than ever to share their collective histories and stories. In recent years, there has been an increase in the number and intensity of anti-Semitic incidents in the United States and around the world. Since the October seventh, twenty twenty-three, Hamas terrorist attack in Israel, there have been more than ten thousand anti-Semitic incidents in the United States. Hate crime statistics collected by the Federal Bureau of Investigation show a sixty-three percent increase in anti-Semitic incidents in the United States from twenty twenty-two to twenty twenty-three. In twenty eighteen, the United States experienced the single deadliest attack against the Jewish community in the history of the United States with the murder of eleven individuals at the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. Especially in a period of rising anti-Semitism, commemorating the liberation of Auschwitz will help all people of the United States develop greater awareness of the Holocaust and knowledge of the horrors brought about by the systematic murder of six million Jews and millions of other innocent individuals by the Nazi regime.

sections.title: House Resolution
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives calls on all people of the United States to remember the one million one hundred thousand innocent victims murdered at the Auschwitz extermination camp as part of the Holocaust, the six million Jews killed during the Holocaust, and all of the victims of the Nazi reign of terror. The House honors the legacy of the survivors of the Holocaust and of the Auschwitz extermination camp. The House calls on the people of the United States to continue to work toward tolerance, peace, and justice and to continue to work to end all genocide and persecution. The House recommits to combating all forms of anti-Semitism.
